answer:
Here is a complete summary of Anthropic's Claude model lineup in 2026, their specifications, and practical guidance on how to deploy them effectively.

### The 2026 Claude Model Lineup: Specs & Pricing

Anthropic’s 2026 pricing reflects a massive shift toward accessibility, most notably with a 67% price drop for the Opus tier compared to earlier generations [1, 2]. The current generation of models includes:

**1. Claude Haiku 4.5 (The Speed & Scale Tier)**
*   **Pricing:** $1.00 input / $5.00 output per million tokens [3].
*   **Context Window:** 200K tokens [3].
*   **Capabilities:** Anthropic's fastest and most cost-efficient model [4]. It matches the coding capabilities of older Sonnet 4 models but operates at lightning speeds [5, 6]. It includes extended thinking, prompt caching, and computer-use capabilities [7, 8]. 

**2. Claude Sonnet 4.6 (The Production Workhorse)**
*   **Pricing:** $3.00 input / $15.00 output per million tokens [3].
*   **Context Window:** 1M tokens. **Notably, Sonnet 4.6 processes the full 1M context window at standard rates**, removing the long-context pricing surcharge that existed on Sonnet 4.5 [3, 9].
*   **Capabilities:** The "production sweet spot" that delivers near-Opus level intelligence at a fraction of the cost [10, 11]. It excels at complex agentic coding, browser automation (scoring 72.5% on OSWorld), and long-document analysis [12-14].

**3. Claude Opus 4.8 (The Flagship Reasoning Tier)**
*   **Pricing:** $5.00 input / $25.00 output per million tokens [3, 15]. (A "Fast Mode" is available for $10/$50 that delivers output 2.5x faster [15]).
*   **Context Window:** 1M tokens (up to 128K output tokens) [16]. 
*   **Capabilities:** The best model for highly complex reasoning, multi-step agentic workflows, and deep scientific or mathematical problem solving [17, 18]. Opus 4.8 features "adaptive thinking" (choosing its own reasoning depth based on task complexity) and is highly reliable for tasks where accuracy and honesty are critical [19-21].
*   *(Note: Opus 4.6 and 4.7 are also active at the exact same price point, but 4.8 is the latest frontier model released in May 2026 [3, 15].)*

*(Beyond the prompt: Anthropic also recently introduced **Claude Fable 5**, a new ultra-premium tier at $10/$50 per MTok built for multi-day, highly autonomous projects [22, 23].)*

***

### Which Model to Use When (In Plain Terms)

To get the most out of Claude, you should stop viewing it as a single AI and start treating it as a tiered system. The rule of thumb in 2026 is **routing tasks based on complexity and cost**.

**When to use Haiku 4.5:**
Use Haiku for high-volume, repetitive, or simple tasks where a wrong answer is cheap to fix [24]. 
*   **Best for:** Light text classification, basic data extraction, routing user requests, document summarization, and acting as a "sub-agent" that reports back to a smarter model [17, 25, 26].

**When to use Sonnet 4.6:**
Sonnet should be your **default model for 80% of your daily work** [10, 27]. Because Sonnet 4.6 performs almost identically to Opus on coding and computer use, it is a waste of money to use Opus for standard feature development [11, 28].
*   **Best for:** Writing code, reviewing pull requests, generating content, building customer service chatbots, and handling day-to-day enterprise workflows [14, 29, 30].

**When to use Opus 4.8:**
Reserve Opus exclusively for the hardest, highest-stakes problems where output quality genuinely changes your business result [18, 30]. 
*   **Best for:** Massive multi-file codebase refactors, graduate-level scientific reasoning, deep financial audits, and long-horizon autonomous agents that must run unattended for hours [18, 30, 31]. 

***

### Concrete Tactics for 2026 Cost Optimization

If you are paying full list price for every Claude API call in 2026, you are overpaying. Here is how enterprise teams are cutting costs:

*   **The 70/20/10 Routing Strategy:** Route 70% of your automated workload to Haiku (cheap extraction), 20% to Sonnet (standard generation/coding), and only 10% to Opus (the hardest edge cases). This single architectural choice can save thousands of dollars a month [32, 33].
*   **Leverage Prompt Caching (90% Off):** If you repeatedly send the same system prompt, rule set, or long document (like a codebase), cache it. Reading cached tokens costs exactly **10% of the base input price** [34, 35]. For example, Sonnet 4.6 cached input reads drop from $3.00 to just $0.30 per million tokens [36]. 
*   **Use the Batch API for Non-Urgent Tasks (50% Off):** If your workload isn't latency-sensitive (e.g., overnight report generation, bulk data enrichment, or evaluating past chat logs), use the Batch API. It returns results within 24 hours and **slashes all input and output costs by exactly 50% across every model** [37-39].

It remains a strong choice and an important migration reference. If you adopted Opus 4.7 in production, 4.8 is a drop-in upgrade at the same price, but 4.7 is still a highly capable model in its own right.

The reason is cost-efficiency. Sonnet 4.6 scores 79.6% on SWE-bench — a 1.2-point gap that is the smallest in Claude's history . At $3/$15 per million tokens versus Opus's $5/$25, Sonnet delivers nearly identical code quality for 40% less money and faster output.

Claude Opus is Anthropic's premium intelligence tier, optimized for complex reasoning, multi-file code refactoring, and agentic workflows.[2, 10] The release of Claude Opus 4.8 on May 28, 2026, introduced significant improvements in model honesty and reliability.[10, 11] Anthropic reports that Opus 4.8 is roughly four times less likely than Opus 4.7 to let bugs or logical errors in code pass unremarked.[11]
Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5 \ Anthropic

The capabilities of models like Fable 5 and Mythos 5 have the potential to do profound good for the world. We've seen the beginnings of this in Project Glasswing, where the models have helped cyber defenders secure critically important software. We've also seen it in life sciences research, where the models are positing novel hypotheses and speeding up the development of new therapeutics.

Use Opus for:

Spot on for any workload that isn't latency-sensitive. If your pipeline currently processes documents synchronously during business hours, switching to a nightly batch job with caching enabled is probably the single biggest cost reduction you can make this week.

Classification & Data Extraction (70%): Routed to Claude Haiku 4.5 .[14] For simple extraction, validation, and classification tasks, the reasoning difference between Haiku and larger models is negligible, but Haiku is 25 times cheaper than Opus-tier models.[14]

Cache writes: 5-min = 1.25× input, 1-hr = 2× input. Cache hits = 0.10× input (90% discount). Batch processing = 50% off all token rates. Fast Mode on Opus 4.8 = 2× standard rates ($10/$50 per MTok). Fast Mode on Opus 4.7 = 6× standard rates ($30/$150 per MTok).

Opus 4.7 is Anthropic's latest flagship, designed for complex reasoning and agentic workflows. At $5/$25, it's a 67% price drop from the previous Opus 4.1 generation ($15/$75). Note: Opus 4.7 uses a revised tokenizer that may consume up to 35% more tokens for the same text: factor this into cost estimates. Opus 4.6 remains available at the same price.
For the most current pricing information, visit claude.com/pricing .
